steps.title: Identify the form of f(x) from the vertical asymptote
steps.content:
The graph shows a vertical asymptote at $x = -4$. For a function $f(x) = \frac{a}{x+b}$, the vertical asymptote occurs when the denominator equals zero: $x + b = 0$, so $x = -b$.

If the asymptote is at $x = -4$, then $-b = -4$, which gives $b = 4$.

Therefore, $f(x) = \frac{a}{x+4}$ for some constant $a$.

steps.title: Find the constant a using a point on the graph
steps.content:
Reading from the graph at $x = -2$, the curve passes through $y = 3$. Substituting this point into $f(x) = \frac{a}{x+4}$:
$$3 = \frac{a}{-2+4} = \frac{a}{2}$$
Solving: $a = 6$. Therefore, $f(x) = \frac{6}{x+4}$.

steps.title: Apply the transformation g(x) = f(x+4)
steps.content:
The function $g(x) = f(x+4)$ means we substitute $(x+4)$ in place of $x$ in the formula for $f$:
$$g(x) = f(x+4) = \frac{6}{(x+4)+4} = \frac{6}{x+8}$$
That matches choice C — C is correct.
